behaviors.description: The generated function signature matches the expected interface (parameter names, types, return type) specified in the description.
behaviors.justification: The real failure is that the LLM misinterprets the natural language specification and generates a function with wrong parameter names, types, or return type. Mocking the LLM to always return correctly-formatted code would hide this failure—you'd never discover that the real model ignores specific type constraints or parameter names in the description. Only calling a real LLM with varied specifications catches the case where it generates a mismatch. The parsing/extraction logic is straightforward; the bottleneck is LLM instruction-following.
behaviors.test_description: Call the LLM to generate code from a natural language description that specifies exact parameter names and types (e.g., 'write a function `calculate_sum(numbers: List[int]) -> int` that sums all numbers'). Parse the generated code and verify the function signature has the exact parameter names, types, and return type specified. Test with multiple varying specifications to catch LLM drift.

behaviors.description: The generated implementation passes all provided test cases on the first or a subsequent attempt.
behaviors.justification: The real failure is twofold: (a) the LLM generates code that doesn't pass tests initially, and (b) the refinement loop fails to improve the output even after iterations. Mocking the LLM to always return passing code hides both failures. The test execution itself is deterministic, but the LLM's ability to produce or refine code to pass tests depends on the real model's behavior and reasoning. If subprocess isolation is required (typical for safety), the minimum level could be System, but the primary bottleneck is LLM output quality across refinement iterations.
behaviors.test_description: Provide the LLM with a function description and a complete test suite. Allow up to 3 generation/refinement attempts. Verify the generated code passes all test cases either on the first attempt or after refinement. The test suite should include cases that typically require multiple attempts (off-by-one logic, edge case handling) to verify refinement actually improves output.

behaviors.description: When tests fail, the refinement step includes the failure messages in the next LLM prompt so the model can learn from its mistakes.
behaviors.justification: The real failure is a mechanical/engineering issue: the system doesn't capture failure messages, formats them incorrectly, or doesn't pass them to the next prompt. You can verify this by running actual tests (so they fail with real error output), mocking the LLM to capture its input arguments, and inspecting that the failure message is present and correctly formatted. The mock LLM doesn't need to be real—you're testing the message-passing pipeline, not the LLM's ability to learn from mistakes. Multiple components interact (test runner, error capture, prompt construction) but all are in-process and deterministic.
behaviors.test_description: Create a test case that will fail (e.g., assertion error, exception). Run code generation, let it fail tests, mock the LLM to capture the full prompt on the second call, and verify the failure message (stack trace, assertion error) is included in that prompt verbatim. Test multiple failures in sequence, verifying each new prompt includes the prior error message.

behaviors.description: The service stops after 3 failed attempts and returns a structured error with all three attempts and their test results.
behaviors.justification: The real failure is control-flow and response-structure logic: the service doesn't count attempts correctly, continues past 3 attempts, or returns malformed/incomplete results. You can test this with a mocked LLM that consistently generates code that fails tests (deterministic failure), then verify the service stops and returns all three attempts with proper structure. Test execution must be real (so tests actually fail and errors are captured), but the LLM can be mocked because you only need consistent failure, not real refinement behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the LLM to return syntactically invalid or import-broken code. Run the service with a test suite. Verify it attempts exactly 3 times, stops, and returns a JSON response containing all three attempts with (attempt_number, generated_code, error_message, test_results). Verify the structure is consistent and complete for each attempt.

behaviors.description: The full loop — generate, test, refine, test again — produces a working implementation for a function that a single LLM call typically gets wrong.
behaviors.justification: The real failure is that the multi-step refinement pipeline doesn't actually improve the LLM's output—either the prompt format breaks the LLM's ability to fix bugs, or the loop doesn't accumulate feedback properly. Testing this requires running multiple LLM calls in sequence (generate → test → refine → test → repeat), where each step depends on real LLM output and the results of prior steps. A single Agentic call is insufficient; you need the full workflow to completion (up to 3 iterations) to verify the loop's cumulative effect. Mocking the LLM would make this test meaningless.
behaviors.test_description: Select a function specification that single LLM calls typically fail on (e.g., a complex dynamic programming problem, or a function with subtle off-by-one requirements). Run the full service with up to 3 refinement iterations. Verify the final generated code passes all test cases. Run this test multiple times (10+) to account for LLM variance, and log which iteration produced the passing solution. Document whether single-call LLM baseline actually fails, confirming the loop adds value.
